item.title: Chinese AI Firms’ Newest Battleground: World Cup Predictions
item.description: The companies are using interest in the popular sporting event to promote their AI models

item.title: Rate Hike Gives ECB Sense of Confidence
item.description: In 2022, the Sintra forum had embarrassment in the air. Now policymakers are more relaxed.

item.title: China’s Factories Show Resilience Weathering Iran War Turmoil
item.description: Beijing is pursuing tech innovations and high-end production to maintain an edge

item.title: AI Notetakers in Meetings Raise Mounting Privacy Concerns
item.description: Executives and companies are grappling with how to manage the tools’ often uninvited presence.

item.title: Traders on High Alert as the Yen Hits a Four-Decade Low
item.description: The Japanese government has already tried once to stop the yen’s slide. Will it intervene again?

item.title: Lee’s $880 Billion AI Bet Ties Legacy to South Korea Chip Boom
item.description: With South Korea already one of the biggest winners of the global AI boom, President Lee Jae Myung is now staking his legacy on a plan to transform the nation’s less developed southwest into a global chip hub.

item.title: India Plans to Cut Reliance on Middle East Oil After War Shock
item.description: India’s state oil refiners are planning to reduce their reliance on the Middle East following the supply shock of the Iran war, and will lean more heavily on alternative options including through spot-market purchases, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: Weak Hiring Is Hurting Young Workers More than AI, Study Says
item.description: A shortage of job openings in the US is playing a much bigger role in young people’s unemployment than their lack of artificial intelligence skills, according to a new study from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

item.title: Equirus Is Said to Consider Fundraising at $600 Million Value
item.description: Equirus Capital Pvt is considering raising as much as $60 million in a funding round, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: SAP Gives AI Product Oversight to Top Executives in Reshuffle
item.description: SAP SE is divvying up responsibilities for its product and engineering functions in its second top-level reorganization this year, people familiar with the matter said, as Europe’s largest software company grapples with staying ahead of rising artificial intelligence competition.

item.title: Bangladesh Central Bank Holds Rate as Risks Linger
item.description: Bangladesh’s central bank kept its benchmark interest rate unchanged on Tuesday, opting for caution amid lingering external risks.

item.title: ECB Officials Stay Alert as Lane Shifts Focus to Price Pipeline
item.description: European Central Bank officials insisted the inflation shock caused by the Iran war can’t yet be judged to be over, even if hostilities have largely ceased.

item.title: Chip Stocks’ Best Quarter Ever Is Ending With Some Wild Swings
item.description: Chip stocks are heading for their best quarter ever, extending an extraordinary start to the year driven by insatiable demand for artificial intelligence equipment. But after recent jitters sent the stocks tumbling, investors are wondering how much further the rally can go.

item.title: The Original Prediction Market Boosters Have Qualms About the Boom
item.description: At Manifest, some tech intellectuals wondered whether Super Bowl bets were really helping humanity.

item.title: What’s at Stake for Beef as Flesh-Eating Screwworm Cases Exceed Two Dozen
item.description: Ranchers, veterinarians and government agencies are battling the invasive US screwworm infestation on multiple fronts. Will it be enough to contain the spread?

item.title: Apps, Networking, Black Market: How Russians Battle Fuel Crunch
item.description: Ukraine’s campaign of attacks on Russian oil refineries is increasingly complicating the lives of drivers across the country as they are forced to embark on more difficult quests to refuel their cars.

item.title: Why the US-Mexico-Canada Trade Deal Is in Jeopardy
item.description: Since the first free trade agreement between the US, Canada and Mexico took effect in 1994, the three countries have established one of the world’s most formidable trading blocs. Their current pact, the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA), creates a common market for goods and services for some 515 million people whose combined economies account for 30% of the world’s gross domestic product.

item.title: Global Central Banks Plan to Reduce Dollar Holdings, Survey Says
item.description: For the first time in three years, global central banks plan to reduce their exposure to the US dollar in the long term, according to a UK-based think tank.

item.title: What the Saga Over Anthropic’s Mythos Tells Us About the Cyber Risks From AI
item.description: Anthropic PBC has said its Mythos artificial intelligence model is so good at finding vulnerabilities in software and computer systems that it can’t be released to the general public. As of early June, the AI giant had released Mythos to only 200 partner organizations because if a tool this powerful fell into the wrong hands, Anthropic said, it could help attackers more easily steal data or disrupt critical infrastructure.

item.title: Ethiopia Bondholders Criticise IMF for ‘Poorly’ Handled Debt Rework
item.description: Ethiopia’s creditors took a swipe at the International Monetary Fund’s handling of the nation’s debt-restructuring process, describing the past two years of discussions as “poorly” managed.

item.title: Japan Food Price Hikes to Jump in July, Continue Through Summer
item.description: The prices of a total of 2,566 food and beverage items in Japan will be subject to increases in July, the most since April, according to a regular survey by Teikoku Databank.

item.title: Slok Says ECB Hike in September Is Possible Despite Energy Drop
item.description: The European Central Bank will probably still consider raising interest rates in September even after a substantial decline in energy prices since its last meeting, according to Torsten Slok, chief economist of Apollo Global Management Inc.

item.title: Segantii Trial Enters Endgame as Defense Wraps Up Testimony
item.description: The insider trading trial of Segantii Capital Management came to a halt on Tuesday, following more than a month of testimony that put a magnifying glass on a tiny series of trades that took place almost a decade ago.

item.title: Takaichi’s New BOJ Board Pick Signals Tame View on Inflation
item.description: New Bank of Japan Board Member Ayano Sato said the country’s inflation views aren’t very strong yet, suggesting her tilt toward accommodative policy as an appointee of Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi.

item.title: EU Cuts Tariff-Free Steel Quotas 33% For Close Trade Partners
item.description: The European Union will reserve half of its steel quotas for countries with free-trade deals under new measures meant to protect local industry from mostly Chinese imports.

item.title: Citi’s Chew Sees Selloff Risk in Tech Stocks From Positioning
item.description: US technology shares are at risk of further declines as overall investor exposure to the cohort is still elevated, according to Citigroup Inc. strategists.

item.title: Rand Trading Surges as War Spawns Volatility, Central Bank Says
item.description: Trading in the rand rose sharply in the first quarter as volatility induced by the Iran war pushed up average daily turnover by as much as 20%, according to the South African Reserve Bank.

item.title: India May Defy Slow Start to Reach Another IPO Record: Kotak's Jayasankar
item.description: India’s equity capital markets are on track for another record year despite a slower start to the year for initial public offerings, according to Kotak Investment Banking, which expects a listings boom in the second half.

item.title: Japan Nears Longest Postwar Growth Even as Households Struggle
item.description: Japan is close to notching its longest economic growth streak since WWII, as the nation weathers higher oil prices from the war in Iran and the central bank’s ongoing quest to normalize interest rates.

item.title: NeoPulse-Led Group to Acquire Stake in Korean Game Maker Wemade
item.description: A consortium led by NeoPulse is close to a deal to buy nearly 40% of South Korean firm Wemade Co. from founder and Chairman Park Kwan-ho, a spokesman for Wemade said in response to a Bloomberg News query.
